name: San Cipriano-Montejurra – circular from the Monastery of Santa María la Real de Iratxe
name_original: San Cipriano-Montejurra – circular desde el Monasterio de Santa María la Real de Iratxe
region_intro: Hiking around Ayegui offers diverse landscapes in Navarre, Spain, characterized by its position near Montejurra Mountain and the Camino de Santiago. The terrain includes a mix of flat areas, challenging ascents to mountain peaks, and moderate excursions through varied natural features. This region provides a range of hiking trails suitable for different skill levels, from gentle paths to more demanding routes. Best hiking trails around Ayegui The most popular hiking route is San Cipriano-Montejurra – circular from the Monastery of Santa María la Real de Iratxe, a 6.2 miles (9.9 km) trail that takes 3 hours 35 minutes to complete. This moderate route includes significant elevation gain towards Montejurra. Another top favourite among local hikers is Montejurra Summit – Montejurra loop from Monasterio de Santa María la Real de Irache, a moderate 7.7 miles (12.4 km) path. This trail offers panoramic views from the summit of Montejurra. Local hikers also love the PlazaSantiago – San Pedro de la Rúa loop from Monasterio de Santa María la Real de Irache, an 8.5 miles (13.6 km) trail leading through varied landscapes, often completed in about 3 hours 41 minutes. Hiking around Ayegui is defined by mountain ascents, historical pilgrimage paths, and diverse natural features. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to more challenging climbs. The routes in Ayegui are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 50 reviews. More than 600 hikers have used komoot to explore Ayegui's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Ayegui?
A: Ayegui offers a wide variety of hiking opportunities, with over 45 trails available. These routes cater to different skill levels, including easy walks, moderate excursions, and more challenging mountain ascents.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes near Ayegui?
A: The terrain around Ayegui is quite diverse. You'll find a mix of flat areas, especially closer to the village and Estella, alongside challenging ascents to mountain peaks like Montejurra. Many trails also follow sections of the historic Camino de Santiago, offering varied landscapes.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly hiking routes in Ayegui?
A: Yes, Ayegui has a good selection of easier trails. There are 19 routes classified as easy, perfect for beginners or those looking for a more relaxed walk. These typically feature less elevation gain and shorter distances.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or attractions I can see while hiking in Ayegui?
A: Hikers in Ayegui can encounter several interesting landmarks. Along the Camino de Santiago, you might pass the unique &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/382711" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Irache Fountain&lt;/a&gt;, famous for dispensing free wine, and the historic Monastery of Irache. The nearby town of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3745111" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Estella-Lizarra&lt;/a&gt; also offers cultural sights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7387615" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iglesia de San Pedro de la Rúa&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes around Ayegui?
A: Yes, many routes around Ayegui are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e965915825/san-cipriano-montejurra-circular-from-the-monastery-de-santa-maria-la-real-de-iratxe" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Cipriano-Montejurra – circular from the Monastery of Santa María la Real de Iratxe&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate loop that takes you towards Montejurra.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Ayegui?
A: While specific regulations can vary, many natural areas in Navarre are generally dog-friendly, especially if your dog is kept on a leash. It's always recommended to check local signage or specific trail guidelines before heading out to ensure a pleasant experience for everyone.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking options in Ayegui?
A: Ayegui offers several routes suitable for families, particularly among its 19 easy trails. These paths are generally shorter and have less challenging terrain, making them ideal for hikers of all ages. Look for routes with minimal elevation gain for the most family-friendly experience.

Q: Where can I park when hiking near Montejurra or other trails in Ayegui?
A: Parking is generally available in Ayegui itself, particularly near the Monastery of Santa María la Real de Irache, which serves as a common starting point for many trails, including those leading to Montejurra. You can also find parking options in nearby Estella-Lizarra for routes originating from there.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy the most about hiking in Ayegui?
A: The hiking trails around Ayegui are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 50 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse landscapes, the panoramic views from Montejurra, and the unique experience of walking sections of the Camino de Santiago.

Q: What is the best time of year to hike in Ayegui?
A: Spring and autumn are generally considered the best times to hike in Ayegui. The weather is milder, and the natural scenery is particularly vibrant. Summers can be hot, especially at midday, while winters can bring colder temperatures and occasional snow on higher elevations like Montejurra.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers?
A: Yes, for experienced trekkers, Ayegui offers several challenging routes. There are 6 trails classified as difficult, often involving significant elevation changes and rugged terrain. These routes provide a rewarding experience with stunning views, particularly those ascending Montejurra.

Q: Can I reach the Montejurra summit from Ayegui?
A: Absolutely. Montejurra is Ayegui's natural emblem, and there are multiple routes to its summit. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/15001163" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montejurra Summit – Montejurra loop from Monasterio de Santa María la Real de Irache&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate option that offers panoramic views from the top.
tips:
The Monastery of Irache (in Basque: Iratxeko monasterioa, the spelling Iratxe is most commonly used in both languages) is a monastic complex located in the Navarrese town of Ayegui in Spain. Its origins date back to the eighth century and some of the current buildings keep parts from the eleventh century. It was run by the Benedictines.

It is one of the most important monumental complexes of the autonomous community of Navarre in which the Romanesque church of the twelfth century, the Plateresque cloister and the Herrerian tower stand out. It is located on the side of the Camino de Santiago and near Estella.
https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monasterio_de_Santa_Mar%C3%ADa_la_Real_de_Irache
From this panoramic point you can see Montejurra and San Cipriano, two of the highest peaks in the region, which you can reach by picturesque paths starting from this site that has parking for cars.

With its top at 1042 meters above sea level. San Cipriano is next to Montejurra one of the most important and visited peaks in the area and from where you will have beautiful views. You can get there by picturesque paths from the town of Iratxe on a very colorful circular route.

It is without a doubt the top to visit if you are walking through the Iratxe area. With an altitude of 1045 meters above sea level. the views are impressive and you can enjoy a beautiful landscape. It is next to the San Cipriano and towards the opposite side is the geodesic vertex of the same name. A place worth visiting!

At this wine well 200l of wine flow out per day for free. When it is empty, it is empty. With a lot of thirst, it's better to come by earlier this day. :)

But then always look nice for the way, otherwise it may be longer (for me it was 46.2 km that day).
